insider-trading.title: Insider Trading — When the Boss Bets on Himself
insider-trading.subtitle: Why CEO stock purchases are the most underrated signal in investing

sections.heading: What Is Insider Trading (The Legal Kind)?
sections.body:
"Insider trading" sounds illegal, but the legal version is totally normal and actually one of the best signals in investing.

**Legal insider trading:** When a CEO, director, or officer of a company buys or sells their OWN company's stock. They're required by law to report it within 2 business days on a form called **Form 4**. It's public record on EDGAR.

**Illegal insider trading:** When someone trades based on material non-public information (like knowing a merger is happening before it's announced). That's the kind that sends people to prison.

We only track the legal kind. The form 4 filings. The ones where the boss is putting their own money on the line.

sections.heading: Why Do Insiders Trade?
sections.body:
There are two types of insider activity:

**Buying (BULLISH signal):**
When a CEO buys their own stock, they're saying "I think this company is going up." They know the business better than any Wall Street analyst. They see the quarterly numbers before they're public. They know about new products, cost savings, upcoming deals.

Academic research shows insider buying beats the market by **6-8% per year** on average.

**Selling (less informative):**
Insiders sell for all kinds of reasons — diversification, college tuition, buying a house, pre-planned sales (10b5-1 forms). Selling doesn't necessarily mean bad news.

**Cluster buying (STRONGEST signal):**
When 3+ insiders buy at the same time? That's a cluster buy. It's one of the strongest signals in finance. Multiple insiders all voting with their wallets simultaneously = they all know something good is coming.

sections.heading: The Research
sections.body:
Studies going back decades show:

- **Seyhun (1986):** Insiders earn ~5% abnormal returns on their purchases
- **Jeng et al. (2003):** Insider purchases outperform by 6.2% annually
- **Cohen et al. (2012):** "Informative" buys (not routine) show even stronger returns

The pattern: Insiders who buy aggressively (large amounts relative to their holdings) show the strongest future stock performance.

**Why doesn't everyone just copy insiders?**
1. Most people don't know how to read Form 4 filings
2. The data is buried in EDGAR's messy index files
3. There's a lot of noise (routine sales, tax-related selling)
4. The edge is small — 6-8% annually — so it's not a get-rich-quick scheme

That's exactly why we filter and score it for you.

sections.heading: The System
sections.body:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              INSIDER TRADING DETECTION                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│  CEO/Director buys $500K of company stock                │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Form 4 filed within 48 hours (PUBLIC on EDGAR)          │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Our bot scans EDGAR index every 3 hours                 │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Is this a CLUSTER? (3+ insiders same period)            │
│         │                                                │
│    ┌────┴────┐                                           │
│    │ YES     │ NO                                        │
│    ▼         ▼                                           │
│  STRONG    NORMAL                                        │
│  SIGNAL    SIGNAL                                        │
│  (Score 8+) (Score 4-7)                                  │
│    │         │                                           │
│    ▼         ▼                                           │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  YOUR DASHBOARD:                     │               │
│  │  • Company name + ticker             │               │
│  │  • Who bought + their title          │               │
│  │  • How much they spent               │               │
│  │  • Price per share                   │               │
│  │  • Cluster? (yes/no)                 │               │
│  │  • Difficulty + Capital needed       │               │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

sections.heading: How to Trade Insider Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy**
**Capital needed: $100-$1K**
**Timeframe: 1-6 months**

The playbook:
1. Look for cluster buys (3+ insiders) — strongest signal
2. Check the size of the purchase relative to their holdings
3. CEO purchases > Director purchases (CEOs know more)
4. Buy when insiders buy, hold for 3-6 months
5. Set stop losses — insiders can be wrong too

**Red flags to watch for:**
- Insider buying but company is simultaneously issuing bad news (could be a distraction)
- Very small purchases (token amounts just for optics)
- Selling right after buying (look at the full picture)

**The insider trading edge isn't about one trade. It's about a strategy of consistently following smart money over time.**

going-concern.title: Going Concern Warnings — The Nuclear Red Flag
going-concern.subtitle: When the SEC says a company might not survive

sections.heading: What Is a Going Concern Warning?
sections.body:
In accounting, there's a basic assumption called the "going concern" principle. It assumes a company will keep operating for the foreseeable future — at least the next 12 months.

When auditors or the SEC determine this assumption is no longer valid, they require the company to disclose it. This is a **going concern warning**.

Translation: **"The SEC/auditors think this company might run out of money within a year."**

This is the most serious red flag in corporate finance. It's not about performance — it's about survival.

sections.heading: The Statistics Are Terrifying
sections.body:
Companies that receive going concern warnings:
- **80% file for bankruptcy within 2 years** (Journal of Accounting Research)
- Average stock price drop on disclosure: **30-60%** within 30 days
- Companies with **multiple** going concern warnings are even worse
- **Remediation rate** (company survives past 2 years): only ~20%

The 80% bankruptcy rate is not a suggestion. It's a warning that should never be ignored.

sections.heading: Famous Examples
sections.body:
**Enron (2001)**: Going concern language appeared in filings months before the full fraud was exposed. Stock went from $90 to $0.61. If you had read the filings, you could have saved yourself.

**Lehman Brothers (2008)**: While not a formal going concern warning, the liquidity crisis was the same concept. Stock went from $85 to $0. The warning signs were there in the repo 105 accounting tricks.

**Hertz (2020)**: Going concern disclosed in April 2020 (during COVID). Filed for bankruptcy in May 2020. Took 1 month.

**Wirecard (2020)**: The German fintech darling. Going concern questions emerged about their cash. Stock dropped **99% in 3 days** when the €1.9 billion was revealed as missing.

**J.Crew, Neiman Marcus, JCPenney (2020)**: All had going concern disclosures before bankruptcy filings.

sections.heading: How to Spot Them Before the Warning
sections.body:
Warning signs that a going concern warning is coming:
- **Negative cash flow from operations** (company burning cash)
- **High debt coming due** (can't refinance)
- **Recurring operating losses** (multiple years of losses)
- **Current ratio below 1** (more liabilities than assets)
- **Covenant violations** (breaking loan terms)
- **Auditor resignation** (auditors don't want to be associated with it)
- **Going low on cash** (cash declining quarter after quarter)
- **Customer concentration** (one big customer could kill them)

If you see 3+ of these signals, a going concern warning might be coming soon.

sections.heading: What to Do When You See One
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy (if selling)**
**Timeframe: Days — act quickly**

Immediate actions:
1. **Read the filing** — understand WHY they're warning
2. Is it temporary (one-time charge) or structural (burning cash runway)?
3. **If structural**: Sell. Don't wait for the "bottom." With an 80% bankruptcy rate, you can't time it.
4. **If temporary**: Monitor closely, but understand that "temporary" often becomes permanent.
5. Check if the company has a plan — new financing, asset sales, restructuring
6. **Shorting is risky** — if there's a buyout rumor, you could get squeezed

The math is simple: If you own stock in a company with a going concern warning and you wait to see what happens, you have a 20% chance of keeping your money. Would you walk across a bridge that had an 80% chance of collapsing?

potential-earnings.title: Potential Earnings — What Can You Actually Make?
potential-earnings.subtitle: Realistic math on signal-based trading profits

sections.heading: The Honest Truth
sections.body:
Let's be real: "potential earnings" on our signal cards are **optimistic scenarios**. They're what could happen IF everything plays out perfectly. In reality, you'll rarely capture the full upside.

A good rule: **take profits at 50% of the indicated potential.** Greed is how beginners lose money.

Here's the realistic breakdown by signal type:

sections.heading: SEC Comment Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 5-40% upside**
**Timeframe: 2-8 weeks**
**Difficulty: Easy to Medium**
**Capital: $100-$10K**

Realistic scenario:
- High materiality comment (score 8+): Stock drops 10-30% over 2-4 weeks
- If you short: capture 10-20% profit (not the full drop — you won't get perfect timing)
- If you stay away: you avoid the drop, which is itself a win

**Win rate:** About 55-60% of high-materiality comments lead to meaningful stock declines within 8 weeks

**Key insight:** You don't need to predict every outcome. You need an edge that compounds over many trades.

sections.heading: Patent Cliff Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 20-80% (short the brand name)**
**Timeframe: 6-18 months**
**Difficulty: Medium to Hard**
**Capital: $1K-$10K+**

Realistic scenario:
- Brand-name stock drops 20-40% as cliff approaches
- Generic company stock rises 10-30% as it prepares to launch
- The biggest moves happen 6-12 months BEFORE the actual cliff

**Win rate:** About 65-70% of major patent cliffs result in significant brand-name revenue declines

**Key insight:** This is a longer-term play. Patience is required. The market is often slow to price in known cliffs.

sections.heading: Insider Trading Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 5-30% over 6 months**
**Timeframe: 1-6 months**
**Difficulty: Easy**
**Capital: $100-$1K**

Realistic scenario:
- Cluster buys (3+ insiders): Stock rises 5-15% over 3-6 months
- Single large insider buy: Stock rises 3-8% over 3-6 months
- The edge is small but consistent

**Win rate:** About 55-60% for cluster buys, 52-55% for individual buys

**Key insight:** Insider trading signals work best as part of a diversified strategy, not as a standalone strategy.

sections.heading: The Math of Compounding
sections.body:
Here's how the math works:

If you have a 55% win rate and you make 10% on winners, lose 5% on losers:
- 100 trades: 55 wins × 10% = +550%
- 100 trades: 45 losses × 5% = -225%
- Net: +325% over 100 trades
- Per trade average: +3.25%

That's a **massive** edge. Most professional fund managers would kill for +3% per trade.

**The secret isn't picking winners. It's managing risk so your winners outpace your losers.**

This is why we show you:
- Difficulty level (so you know what you're getting into)
- Minimum capital (so you don't overextend)
- Chance of fail (so you size your positions correctly)

trading-guide.title: How to Actually Trade These Signals
trading-guide.subtitle: A step-by-step playbook for turning intelligence into profit

sections.heading: Step 1: Understand What You're Trading
sections.body:
You're not trading "tips." You're trading **information asymmetry**.

The information is public. But most people don't read it. That's your edge.

When you see a signal on our dashboard, you're seeing something that:
- Is legally public (on EDGAR)
- Required significant effort to produce (the SEC reviewed the filing)
- Is scored for materiality (our system filtered out the noise)
- Is actionable (you can actually do something with it)

This is not insider information. This is **superior processing of public information**.

sections.heading: Step 2: Assess the Signal
sections.body:
For each signal, check:

**Materiality Score (1-10):**
- 8-10: Serious SEC concern. High probability of market impact.
- 5-7: Moderate concern. May or may not move the stock.
- 1-4: Routine SEC review. Probably nothing.

**Difficulty Level:**
- Easy: Buy/sell stock on Robinhood. Anyone can do this.
- Medium: Options trading. Requires approval + knowledge.
- Hard: Shorting. Requires margin account + understanding of borrow costs.

**Capital Needed:**
- Don't overextend. A good rule: never risk more than 5% of your total portfolio on one signal.

**Timeframe:**
- Don't expect overnight moves. Give signals time to play out.
- Set a reminder to check back in the indicated timeframe.

sections.heading: Step 3: The Trade
sections.body:
**For SEC Comment Signals (Bearish):**
1. Read the comment letter (linked on the card)
2. Assess: Is this serious or routine?
3. If serious → Consider shorting or buying puts
4. If routine → The stock might actually be a buy (overreaction)
5. Set a stop loss at 5-10% above entry
6. Take profits at 50% of indicated potential

**For Patent Cliff Signals (Bearish on brand, bullish on generics):**
1. Identify the cliff date
2. Check if it's already priced in (has the stock already dropped?)
3. If not priced in → Short the brand name or buy puts
4. Alternatively → Long the generic company expected to launch
5. Hold for 6-18 months

**For Insider Trading Signals (Bullish):**
1. Verify it's a cluster buy (3+ insiders)
2. Check the purchase size (larger = stronger signal)
3. Buy the stock
4. Hold for 3-6 months
5. Set a stop loss at 5-10% below entry

sections.heading: Step 4: Risk Management
sections.body:
This is the most important step. Seriously.

**The Golden Rules:**
1. **Never invest more than you can afford to lose**
2. **Always use stop losses** — 5-10% on longs, 5-10% on shorts
3. **Diversify** — Don't bet everything on one signal
4. **Take profits early** — 50% of potential is the target
5. **Don't fall in love with positions** — If the thesis changes, exit

**Position Sizing:**
- High materiality (8-10): Max 10% of portfolio
- Medium materiality (5-7): Max 5% of portfolio
- Low materiality (1-4): Max 2% of portfolio

**The Math:**
If you risk 5% per trade and have a 55% win rate:
- 10 losses in a row: -50% (devastating but recoverable)
- 20 trades with 55% win rate: +10% net
- 50 trades with 55% win rate: +25% net
- 100 trades with 55% win rate: +50% net

**Patience + Math = Profit**

edgar.title: EDGAR — The SEC's Public Database
edgar.subtitle: What it is, why it exists, and why it matters

sections.heading: What is EDGAR?
sections.body:
EDGAR stands for **Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval**. It is the SEC's free public database where every public company in the United States is required to file their financial reports.

The SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) is the federal agency that regulates stock markets. They created EDGAR in 1996 to give the public free access to corporate financial information. Before EDGAR, you had to physically travel to a SEC office in Washington DC and photocopy documents.

Today, EDGAR processes over **200,000 filings per year** and is updated every 10 minutes during trading hours.

sections.heading: Who Uses EDGAR?
sections.body:
- **Hedge funds** pay $100K-$500K/year for premium tools that just read EDGAR faster
- **Investment banks** monitor competitor filings
- **Lawyers** check for regulatory compliance
- **Journalists** investigate corporate wrongdoing
- **Individual investors** (like you) can read the same information for free
- **Academics** study market behavior using EDGAR data

The information is public. Everyone has access. The difference is how fast and how well you can process it.

sections.heading: What Types of Filings Exist?
sections.body:
- **10-K**: Annual report (full financial picture, audited)
- **10-Q**: Quarterly report (unaudited financials)
- **8-K**: Current report (material events within 4 business days)
- **Form 4**: Insider trading reports (CEO/director buys/sells)
- **DEF 14A**: Proxy statement (board elections, executive pay)
- **S-1**: Registration statement (IPO filings)
- **13D/G**: Activist investor disclosures (>5% ownership)
- **CORRESP**: SEC comment letters (the comments + company responses)
- **424B4**: Prospectus (offering documents)

Each filing type tells you something different about the company.

sections.heading: Why EDGAR Matters
sections.body:
EDGAR is the foundation of **transparency** in US financial markets. The idea is simple: if every company must publicly disclose their financials, then investors can make informed decisions. Fraud becomes harder. Insider games become riskier. The playing field gets more level.

The Efficient Market Hypothesis says prices reflect all available information. EDGAR makes sure that information IS available to everyone — not just insiders with Bloomberg terminals.

When we use EDGAR data, we are exercising your right to see what the government requires companies to disclose. It is literally the public record.

sections.heading: How to Navigate EDGAR
sections.body:
The main EDGAR search page: https://www.sec.gov/edgar/searchedgar/companysearch.html

Let's say you want to see everything Apple has filed:
1. Go to EDGAR company search
2. Type "Apple Inc" or ticker "AAPL"
3. You'll see a list of every filing, organized by date
4. Click any filing to read the full document

Pro: It's free. Con: The interface is from 1996 and hard to use.

Our system does the heavy lifting — reads EDGAR, filters for material signals, scores them, and presents them on a dashboard. All the analysis work is done for you.

revenue-recognition.title: Revenue Recognition — How Companies Count Their Money
revenue-recognition.subtitle: Why the timing of revenue recognition can tell you everything

sections.heading: What Is Revenue Recognition?
sections.body:
Revenue recognition is the rule for WHEN a company can officially say they "earned" money.

This sounds simple, but it gets complicated fast:
- Do you count revenue when you sign the contract?
- When you deliver the product?
- When the customer pays?
- When you finish a multi-year project?

Example: You sign a $12 million contract in January to deliver software over 12 months. How much revenue do you report in January?
- The honest answer: $0 (you haven't delivered anything yet)
- The aggressive answer: $12 million (contract is signed!)
- The answer the SEC cares about: depends on when you actually deliver value

**GAAP** (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) provides the rules. **ASC 606** is the current standard for revenue recognition.

sections.heading: Why the SEC Cares
sections.body:
Revenue is the single most important number on any income statement. It tells you how much business a company is doing. If revenue is wrong, everything else is wrong too.

When the SEC questions a company's revenue recognition, they are asking: **"Are you counting money you haven't actually earned yet?"**

This is one of the most common ways companies inflate their performance:
- **Channel stuffing**: Sending retailers more product than they can sell
- **Bill-and-hold**: Recording revenue before delivery
- **Round-trip transactions**: Selling to a related party and buying back
- **Premature recognition**: Booking multi-year contracts all at once

**Enron** was the poster child. They booked revenue from deals that hadn't closed. Their numbers looked incredible on paper, but the cash never arrived.

sections.heading: Red Flags to Watch For
sections.body:
When you see the SEC questioning revenue recognition, look for these patterns:

- **Revenue growing faster than cash flow**: If sales are up 50% but cash is down, something is wrong
- **Accounts receivable growing faster than sales**: Customers aren't paying
- **Deferred revenue declining**: They're eating their backlog
- **Frequent "non-cash" adjustments**: Revenue that never becomes cash is suspicious
- **Changes in recognition policies**: Suddenly counting revenue differently

If you see a comment letter about "timing of revenue recognition" or "when control transfers," pay attention. The SEC may be catching something the market hasn't priced in.

sections.heading: The Technical Detail
sections.body:
ASC 606 uses a 5-step model:
1. Identify the contract
2. Identify the performance obligations
3. Determine the transaction price
4. Allocate the price to obligations
5. Recognize revenue when obligations are satisfied

The key phrase is: **"When control transfers."** Revenue is earned when the CUSTOMER gets control of the product or service, not when the company gets paid.

For software companies this is especially tricky — do you recognize revenue at signup? Over the subscription period? When support is delivered? Each answer changes the financial picture significantly.

sections.heading: How to Trade Revenue Recognition Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Medium**
**Capital needed: $500-$5K**
**Timeframe: 1-3 months**

If you see an SEC comment letter questioning revenue recognition:
1. Read the specific questions — how detailed are they?
2. Did the company respond adequately, or are they dodging?
3. Check their cash flow vs revenue — are they actually getting paid?
4. If the SEC is serious → short or avoid the stock
5. If the SEC accepts the company's explanation → might be a buying opportunity (overreaction)

The key insight: Revenue recognition issues often precede larger problems. Once investors stop trusting the numbers, the stock gets repriced aggressively.

material-weakness.title: Material Weakness — When a Company's Books Can't Be Trusted
material-weakness.subtitle: Internal controls matter more than you think

sections.heading: What Is Internal Control?
sections.body:
Internal controls are the processes a company uses to ensure its financial reports are accurate. Think of it as the system that prevents errors and fraud.

Good internal controls mean:
- Numbers in the financial statements can be trusted
- Employees can't easily steal or cook the books
- The company catches mistakes before they reach investors
- Auditors can verify the numbers

Bad internal controls mean:
- The financial statements might be wrong
- No one would notice if someone manipulated the numbers
- The CEO might lie about revenue and no one would catch it
- Investors are flying blind

**The Sarbanes-Oxley Act** (SOX, 2002) requires public companies to have adequate internal controls. This was created after the Enron scandal showed how easily books could be manipulated.

sections.heading: What Is a Material Weakness?
sections.body:
A material weakness is the WORST category of internal control failure. According to PCAOB (Public Company Accounting Oversight Board):

> "A material weakness is a deficiency, or combination of deficiencies, such that there is a reasonable possibility that a material misstatement of the financial statements will not be prevented, or detected and corrected, on a timely basis."

Translation: **The company's financial systems are broken, and their reported numbers might be significantly wrong.**

This is not a "we found a small error" situation. This is a "we can't trust their financial statements" situation.

sections.heading: How Common Are They?
sections.body:
Material weaknesses are found in about **5-10%** of public companies each year. They are more common in:
- Smaller companies (less resources for compliance)
- Companies going through rapid growth
- Companies with high turnover in finance departments
- Companies that recently acquired other businesses

When a material weakness is disclosed, the stock typically drops **3-8%** on the announcement. If it leads to a restatement, drops can be **20-50%.**

**Famous examples:**
- **Kroger (2021)**: Material weakness in IT controls, restated prior year financials
- **Pennon Group (2018)**: Weakness led to $1.2 billion in errors
- **General Electric (2018)**: Material weakness in power segment, massive restatement

sections.heading: Why the SEC Focuses on This
sections.body:
When the SEC asks about internal controls in a comment letter, they are essentially asking "can we trust what you're telling us?"

Specific questions the SEC might ask:
- "How did you remediate the material weakness disclosed in [prior filing]?"
- "Why did the material weakness persist for multiple reporting periods?"
- "How can investors rely on financial statements if controls are deficient?"

A material weakness is a signal that the company's management either:
a) Doesn't know how to run financial systems properly, OR
b) Knows something is wrong and is hoping no one notices

Neither answer is good for investors.

sections.heading: How to Trade These Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy (for selling)**
**Capital needed: $0 if already holding**
**Timeframe: Days to weeks**

If you see "material weakness" in an SEC comment letter or filing:
- **If you own the stock**: Seriously consider selling. You can't trust their numbers. If their numbers can't be trusted, you can't value the company.
- **If you don't own it**: Avoid. Or short if the weakness is severe and unresolved.
- **If the company says "we're fixing it"**: Skeptical optimism at best. Remediation takes 1-2 years and often fails.
- **If weakness was remediated last year**: Good sign, but monitor. Many companies "fix" the weakness on paper but the problems persist.

**Core principle: Never invest in a company whose financial statements you can't trust. A material weakness means you can't trust them.**

fraud.title: SEC Fraud Detection — When Companies Lie to the Market
fraud.subtitle: How to spot deception before it destroys your portfolio

sections.heading: What Counts as Fraud?
sections.body:
Securities fraud is intentional deception in connection with the sale or purchase of a securities. The SEC pursues several types:

- **Financial statement fraud**: Cooking the books (fake revenue, hidden expenses)
- **Insider trading**: Trading on material non-public information
- **Market manipulation**: Artificially moving stock prices
- **Ponzi schemes**: Paying old investors with new investor money
- **Pump and dump**: Spreading false hype to sell at inflated prices
- **Accounting fraud**: Manipulating financial records

When the SEC brings a fraud enforcement action, it means they have evidence of intentional lying. This is not a mistake. This is deliberate deception.

sections.heading: Famous SEC Fraud Cases
sections.body:
**Enron (2001)**: $74 billion in shareholder value destroyed. Used off-balance-sheet entities to hide debt. Executives sold stock while telling investors everything was fine.

**WorldCom (2002)**: $11 billion accounting fraud. Capitalized operating expenses to inflate profits. CEO Bernard Ebbers went to prison for 25 years.

**Bernie Madoff (2008)**: $65 billion Ponzi scheme. The SEC had received multiple warnings but failed to act for years. Madoff got 150 years in prison.

**Theranos (2018)**: Not a traditional SEC case, but Elizabeth Holmes defrauded investors with fake blood-testing technology. Convicted of fraud.

**Wirecard (2020)**: German fintech. Claimed €1.9 billion existed that didn't. CEO arrested. Company collapsed.

**FTX (2022)**: Crypto exchange. Sam Bankman-Fried transferred customer funds to his trading firm. Convicted on 7 fraud counts.

sections.heading: How the SEC Detects Fraud
sections.body:
The SEC uses several tools:
- **Data analytics**: Unusual patterns in financial statements
- **Whistleblowers**: The SEC pays whistleblowers 10-30% of sanctions over $1M
- **Tips and complaints**: From investors, employees, competitors
- **Market surveillance**: Unusual trading patterns
- **Auditor findings**: When auditors resign or issue bad opinions
- **Media reports**: Investigative journalism often triggers SEC investigations

The SEC's Division of Enforcement has about 1,300 staff. They can't catch everything, but when they do act, the consequences are severe.

sections.heading: What Happens to Fraudsters
sections.body:
- **Criminal charges**: Up to 25 years per count (mail fraud, wire fraud, securities fraud)
- **Civil penalties**: Disgorgement of profits + fines up to $5M per violation
- **Officer/director bars**: Banned from serving as executives of public companies
- **Clawbacks**: Forced to return incentive-based compensation
- **Reputation destruction**: Career-ending, often prison time

The SEC doesn't go after small-time scammers. They focus on cases that send a message to the market. If you see an SEC fraud action, the stock is almost certainly going to zero or near-zero.

sections.heading: How to Protect Yourself
sections.body:
Red flags that might indicate fraud:
- **Revenue growing with no corresponding cash flow**
- **Frequent auditor changes** (auditors don't want to be associated with fraud)
- **Complex related-party transactions** (hiding things in subsidiaries)
- **Management selling stock aggressively** (insiders cashing out)
- **Refusal to answer simple questions** in earnings calls
- **Unrealistic margins** compared to competitors
- **Off-balance-sheet entities** (Enron's playbook)

If you see multiple red flags, the SEC might already be investigating. Check the SEC's litigation releases: https://www.sec.gov/litigation/litreleases.htm

**When in doubt, get out.** If something feels wrong with a company's numbers, trust your gut. You don't need to prove fraud to avoid a stock.

cluster-buy.title: Cluster Buying — When Multiple Insiders Bet Together
cluster-buy.subtitle: The strongest signal in insider trading

sections.heading: What Is a Cluster Buy?
sections.body:
A cluster buy happens when **3 or more insiders** (CEOs, directors, officers) buy their company's stock around the same time.

This is not one person being optimistic. This is multiple people — who all have access to the company's internal information — all deciding to put their own money on the line simultaneously.

It's like 5 chefs at a restaurant all independently deciding to eat there tonight. They know what's really happening in the kitchen.

sections.heading: Why Is It So Powerful?
sections.body:
Individual insider buys can be noise:
- The CEO might buy to signal confidence (even if things are bad)
- A director might buy because they have to (ownership requirements)
- Someone might buy for personal reasons (diversification timing)

But when 3+ insiders buy together:
- It's unlikely to be coincidence
- They all have access to the same internal information
- They're all independently concluding the stock is undervalued
- The signal is much stronger than any individual buy

Research shows cluster buys outperform individual buys by **2-3x** in terms of subsequent returns.

sections.heading: The Research
sections.body:
**Seyhun (1986)**: Found insiders earn ~5% abnormal returns on purchases. Cluster buys showed even stronger results.

**Cohen, Hawawini, Maier, Schwartz, Whitcomb (CHMSW, 2012)**: "The 'informative' insider buys" — those not driven by routine — showed the strongest future stock performance. Cluster buys are the most informative.

**Akbas et al. (2020)**: Found that insider buying clusters predict:
- Future earnings surprises (positive)
- Stock price increases
- Analyst upgrades
- Decreased future stock volatility

The edge is strongest in the **first 30-60 days** after the cluster buy.

sections.heading: How to Spot Cluster Buys
sections.body:
Look for:
- **Same week or month**: Multiple Form 4 filings within days of each other
- **Different insiders**: Not just one person buying more — multiple people buying
- **Meaningful amounts**: Purchases that are significant relative to their holdings
- **Not routine**: Not pre-scheduled 10b5-1 sales (those are planned months in advance)
- **No obvious reason**: Not buying for tax purposes or option exercises

Our system automatically detects and flags cluster buys. When you see "CLUSTER BUY" in the signal keywords, it means multiple insiders bought simultaneously.

sections.heading: How to Trade Cluster Buys
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy**
**Capital needed: $100-$1K**
**Timeframe: 1-6 months**

The playbook:
1. Verify it's a real cluster (3+ insiders, same period)
2. Check the purchase sizes — larger = stronger signal
3. CEO purchases carry more weight than director purchases
4. Buy the stock
5. Hold for 3-6 months
6. Set a stop loss at 5-10% below entry

**Win rate**: About 60-65% for cluster buys
**Average return**: 5-15% over 6 months

**Pro tip**: Cluster buys right before earnings season are especially powerful. The insiders know good news is coming.

form4-filing.title: Form 4 Filings — The Paper Trail of Insider Trades
form4-filing.subtitle: How to read the forms that CEOs must file when they trade

sections.heading: What Is Form 4?
sections.body:
Form 4 is the SEC filing that company insiders (directors, officers, and beneficial owners of more than 10% of shares) must submit whenever they buy or sell their company's stock.

Key rules:
- Must be filed within **2 business days** of the transaction
- It's public record — anyone can read it
- Covers both purchases AND sales
- Must include the price, number of shares, and total holdings after the trade

Form 4 is filed on EDGAR. You can search for any company and see every insider trade ever made.

sections.heading: How to Read a Form 4
sections.body:
A Form 4 contains:
- **Filer name and relationship**: CEO, Director, Officer, 10%+ owner
- **Transaction date**: When the trade happened
- **Transaction type**: Buy, sell, option exercise, tax withholding
- **Number of shares**: How many shares were traded
- **Price per share**: The price they paid/received
- **Total shares owned after**: Their total holdings post-transaction
- **Direct vs indirect**: Whether they own shares personally or through a trust/entity

The most important fields: **Who**, **How much**, **At what price**, and **What % of the company they now own.**

sections.heading: Understanding Transaction Codes
sections.body:
Form 4 uses letter codes for transaction types:
- **P**: Purchase (open market buy)
- **S**: Sale (open market sell)
- **A**: Grant/award (they got stock as compensation)
- **F**: Tax withholding (shares withheld to pay taxes on vesting)
- **M**: Option exercise (converting options to shares)
- **G**: Gift (giving stock away)
- **C**: Conversion (converting one security type to another)

**P and S are what you care about.** A, F, M are usually routine compensation stuff, not signals.

If you see a **P** code from a CEO, that's the CEO buying with their own money. That's the signal.

sections.heading: 10b5-1 Plans — The Loophole
sections.body:
Some insider sales are pre-planned through **10b5-1 plans**. These are trading plans set up in advance (usually during a "trading window" when they don't have material non-public information).

The plan specifies:
- How many shares to sell
- When to sell (dates or price thresholds)
- Can be set up months before the actual sales

**Why this matters:** Sales under 10b5-1 plans are usually NOT signals. The insider planned the sale months ago, before they had current information. It's like setting up automatic reinvestment dividends — it tells you nothing about their current view.

**How to spot them:** The Form 4 will mention "10b5-1" in the footnotes. Our system tries to filter these out.

sections.heading: Where to Find Form 4 Filings
sections.body:
Direct on EDGAR:
https://www.sec.gov/edgar/browse/?CIK=&type=4&dateb=&owner=include&count=40&search_text=&action=getcompany

Or search by company:
https://www.sec.gov/edgar/searchedgar/companysearch.html

Third-party sites that make it easier:
- **OpenInsider.com**: Clean interface for insider trades
- **InsiderMonitor.com**: Tracks unusual insider activity
- **WhaleWisdom.com**: 13F + insider trading data

Our system does the work for you — scans all Form 4 filings, filters for significant ones, scores them, and presents them on your dashboard.

patent-cliff.title: Patent Cliffs — When Drug Monopolies Die
patent-cliff.subtitle: Why knowing expiration dates can make you a fortune

sections.heading: The Basic Concept
sections.body:
When a pharma company invents a new drug, they get a **20-year patent**. That means NOBODY else can make that drug for 20 years. Total monopoly. They can charge whatever they want.

When the patent expires? **Game over for the brand name.** Any company can now make a "generic" version — same drug, same formula, but costs 80-90% less.

This is called a **patent cliff** because brand-name sales FALL OFF A CLIFF when generics enter.

**Exhibit A: Lipitor (Pfizer)**
- Peak sales: **$12.4 BILLION per year** (2006)
- Patent expired: November 2011
- Sales within 1 year: **$2.3 billion**
- That's an **82% drop** in revenue from one drug

**Exhibit B: Humira (AbbVie)**
- Peak sales: **$21 billion per year**
- Patent cliffs started: 2023
- Multiple biosimilar competitors entered
- Revenue expected to drop 30-40% by 2025

**Exhibit C: Keytruda (Merck)**
- World's best-selling drug: **$25 billion/year**
- Patent expires: 2028
- Merck is desperately trying to extend or find replacements

sections.heading: Why Does This Matter to You?
sections.body:
Because the market doesn't always price in patent cliffs immediately. Sometimes there's a 6-18 month window where:
- The cliff is approaching (publicly known)
- Wall Street analysts are still modeling peak sales
- The stock hasn't dropped yet

**If you know a patent cliff is coming, and the market hasn't priced it in, you can short the stock and profit from the crash.**

But it's not that simple:
- Companies try to extend patents (evergreening)
- New indications can extend revenue
- Buyouts and acquisitions can change the math
- Sometimes the cliff is already priced in

**That's why we track this.** We show you what's coming, when, and how severe.

sections.heading: The Patent Cliff Timeline
sections.body:
```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              THE PATENT CLIFF LIFECYCLE                     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 0: Drug Approved                                      │
│  ├── Patent protection begins                               │
│  ├── Monopoly pricing: $10,000/month treatment              │
│  └── Stock rises on blockbuster potential                   │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 10: Mid-life                                          │
│  ├── Sales peak: $10-20 billion/year                        │
│  ├── Analysts love it                                       │
│  └── Patent cliff is "on the radar" but distant             │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 15-18: Cliff Approaching                              │
│  ├── Generics manufacturers file for approval               │
│  ├── Lawsuits begin (patent challenges)                     │
│  ├── Smart money starts shorting                            │
│  └── Stock starts declining                                 │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 20: PATENT EXPLODES                                   │
│  ├── Generics flood the market                              │
│  ├── Brand price drops 50-90%                               │
│  ├── Sales crash 80%                                        │
│  └── Stock drops 20-80%                                     │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 20+: New Normal                                       │
│  ├── Brand survives on loyalty + new formulations           │
│  ├── Revenue stabilizes at 20% of peak                      │
│  └── Company must find new drugs or die                     │
│                                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

sections.heading: How to Trade Patent Cliffs
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Medium to Hard**
**Capital needed: $1K-$10K+**
**Timeframe: 6-18 months**

The playbook:
1. Identify drugs expiring in the next 1-3 years
2. Check the brand-name company's revenue dependence on that drug
3. Look for "cliff hedging" — are they already diversifying?
4. If over-dependent and cliff is close → short opportunity
5. If already priced in → look for generic companies to go LONG

**Generic companies WIN from patent cliffs.** Companies like Teva, Sandoz, and Mylan make their living off these expirations. When a major cliff approaches, generics stock up.

**Pro tip:** The FDA maintains a "Orange Book" — the official list of drug patents and exclusivity. It's public. We cross-reference it with our data.

sec-comment.title: SEC Comment Letters — The Full Breakdown
sec-comment.subtitle: Why the government's letters to companies are basically cheat codes

sections.heading: What Even IS EDGAR?
sections.body:
EDGAR stands for **Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval**. It's the SEC's free public database — basically a massive digital filing cabinet where every public company (Apple, Tesla, GameStop, all of them) is legally required to dump their financial reports.

The SEC is the **Securities and Exchange Commission** — the federal agency that watches over Wall Street like a hall monitor. They were created after the 1929 stock market crash to make sure companies can't scam investors.

EDGAR has existed since 1996. Before that, you had to physically go to a SEC office in Washington DC and photocopy documents. Nightmare.

**Fun fact:** The name "EDGAR" was originally just a project acronym, but it stuck. There's no actual person named Edgar. Sorry.

sections.heading: So What Are Comment Letters?
sections.body:
Here's the flow:

1. A company files their quarterly or annual report on EDGAR
2. SEC staff (called "reviewers") read through it
3. If something looks sketchy, confusing, or incomplete, they send a **comment letter** — basically a formal "hey, explain yourself" letter
4. The company has to respond with clarification
5. Back-and-forth continues until the SEC is satisfied
6. **ALL of this is public record on EDGAR**

Think of it like this: You submit your homework. Your teacher writes "explain question 3" in red pen. You have to write a response. Except this is a billion-dollar company and the teacher is a federal regulator, and the homework is whether they're cooking their books.

sections.heading: Why Should I Care?
sections.body:
Because comment letters are **early warning signals**.

Academic studies have shown that companies receiving certain types of SEC comments are significantly more likely to:
- Restate their earnings later (admitting their numbers were wrong)
- Get sued by investors
- See their stock price drop
- Go bankrupt (in extreme cases, like going-concern comments)

**Exhibit A: The Going Concern Bomb**

If the SEC asks "does this company have enough cash to survive the next 12 months?" — that's a GOING CONCERN warning. It's the nuclear option. The SEC is basically saying "we're not sure this company is going to make it."

Companies that receive going concern warnings have an **80% chance of bankruptcy within 2 years**. That's not a typo. Eighty percent.

**Exhibit B: Revenue Recognition**

If the SEC questions HOW a company counts its revenue, pay attention. This is how Enron fooled everyone for years — they were counting revenue before they actually earned it. Like marking your test as "A+" before taking it.

**Exhibit C: Material Weakness**

When the SEC says a company has "material weakness in internal controls," it means their financial reporting systems are broken. You literally can't trust the numbers they're showing you.

sections.heading: How Hedge Funds Use This
sections.body:
Big hedge funds pay **$100K-$500K/year** for premium tools that basically just read EDGAR faster and add analytics. Services like RavenPack, Sentieo, and others charge insane premiums for what amounts to "we read the SEC comments before you do."

Our system does the same thing for free. The data is public. The SEC publishes it for free. We just automated the boring reading part.

**The edge isn't the data — it's the speed of reading it.** If you see a comment letter before the market fully digests it, you have a window where the stock hasn't moved yet. That's the opportunity.

sections.heading: How to Trade These Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy to Medium**
**Capital needed: $100-$10K**
**Timeframe: 2-8 weeks (high severity) or 1-3 months (medium)**

Step-by-step:
1. Check the signal card for materiality score (7+ = serious)
2. Read the specific questions the SEC asked
3. Look at the company's response (if available)
4. Decide: Is this a real problem or just routine SEC review?
5. If real problem → consider shorting or staying away
6. If routine → the stock might actually be a buy (overreaction)

**Golden rule:** Not every comment letter means doom. The SEC sends hundreds per year. Most are routine. The art is knowing which ones matter. That's what our scoring system does for you.
